scenario: Sable Bank publishes 13000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 from an at-least-once webhook source and developers need to deduplicate the messages so the same payment notification is not correlated twice when the upstream publisher retries the same correlation key.
question: Which Camunda 8 message attribute provides idempotency so the same message is processed at most once per name and correlation key?

options.text: The processInstanceKey on the publish command, which the broker uses to dedupe across instances at the gateway level before correlation happens at all
The timeToLive value, which transparently doubles as a deduplication window for any duplicates that arrive inside that same TTL on the broker
The correlation key alone, with identical correlation keys always rejected by the broker as duplicate correlations within a single process at runtime
The optional message ID, with a message rejected when a message of the same name, correlation key and ID is already buffered on the broker

a.text: Incorrect. Publishing does not target a specific process instance directly; the documented deduplication mechanism is the optional message ID, not a process instance key on the publish command.

b.text: Incorrect. TTL only controls buffering duration; it does not perform any deduplication of messages with the same name and correlation key on its own without a message ID set.

c.text: Incorrect. The correlation key alone is not a deduplication key; the docs require the same name, correlation key and message ID together to trigger rejection of a buffered duplicate.

d.text: Correct. The Message uniqueness section states a message is rejected and not correlated if a message with the same name, the same correlation key and the same ID is already buffered.

explanation: Message uniqueness uses an optional message ID. When set, the broker rejects any new message whose name, correlation key and ID match a message already in the buffer. The uniqueness check is disabled when no message ID is provided on the publish call.
